intro: 1979. Your research lab, Xerox PARC, has quietly invented the future: the graphical interface, the mouse, Ethernet, the laser printer, the personal computer. You run Xerox — a copier company — and headquarters three thousand miles away has no idea what it's sitting on.

realOutcome.valuation: invented the PC and captured almost none of it
realOutcome.summary: Xerox invented modern computing at PARC and let others build the trillion-dollar industry. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: A young Steve Jobs wants to tour PARC and see your graphical interface. Your investment arm can get pre-IPO Apple shares in exchange for showing him around. Your engineers are horrified at what you're about to give away.

choices.text: Trade the demo for a $1M stake in Apple.
choices.reveal: Xerox let Jobs tour PARC in exchange for the right to invest in Apple. He walked out with the ideas that became the Macintosh. Xerox traded the future of computing for a modest stock stake.

choices.text: Keep the interface secret and build it yourself.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You guard the crown jewels and ship the graphical PC first. Xerox, not Apple or Microsoft, defines how the whole world uses computers.

choices.text: License the interface to everyone for royalties.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: If you're going to share it, at least own the standard. Every GUI computer sold pays Xerox a toll, and you profit from an industry you seeded.

decisions.scenario: PARC built the Alto — a real personal computer with a mouse and windows, a decade ahead of everyone. You can bring a version to market, but pricing and packaging it for real people is a fight with your copier-minded company.

choices.text: Ship the Xerox Star at $16,000+ for offices.
choices.reveal: Xerox shipped the Star in 1981 — brilliant, and priced at over $16,000 for a workstation. It flopped commercially while cheaper PCs from Apple and IBM took the market it invented.

choices.text: Ship an affordable personal computer for everyone.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You price the PC for real people instead of only corporations. Xerox owns the personal computer the way it owned the copier.

choices.text: Partner with a PC maker to mass-produce it.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You bring in a partner who knows high-volume consumer manufacturing. PARC's genius meets someone who can actually build and sell it at scale.

decisions.scenario: The mouse and graphical interface could go to the mass market as cheap add-ons for any computer. Or you can keep them locked inside your expensive high-end office equipment.

choices.text: Keep the mouse and GUI in high-end office gear.
choices.reveal: Xerox treated its breakthroughs as premium office features rather than mass-market products. The mouse and GUI conquered the world — on everyone else's machines.

choices.text: Sell a cheap mouse and GUI for every computer.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You put Xerox's interface on every desk in the world for a few dollars each. Ubiquity plus a small margin beats exclusivity on a product nobody can afford.

choices.text: Spin PARC out as its own computer company.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Freed from the copier company's instincts, a standalone PARC moves at the speed of Silicon Valley and commercializes what it invented.

decisions.scenario: The copier executives at headquarters call the computing researchers 'toner heads' and see PARC as a costly hobby. You can leave the company's center of gravity in copiers, or move it toward the future PARC is building.

choices.text: Let HQ stay focused on copiers.
choices.reveal: Xerox's leadership stayed a copier company at heart, treating PARC's work as a distraction. The distance — cultural and literal — between Stamford and Palo Alto let the future slip away.

choices.text: Move the company's center of gravity to PARC.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You reorient Xerox around computing and back the people inventing it. The copier profits fund the transition instead of smothering it.

choices.text: Fund a dedicated West Coast computing division.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You give computing its own budget, leadership, and mandate, insulated from copier politics. PARC's inventions finally get a company that wants them.

decisions.scenario: PARC also invented Ethernet — the way computers talk to each other. You can sit on it and keep milking copiers, or build the networked-office business the whole industry is about to need.

choices.text: Sit on Ethernet and keep milking copiers.
choices.reveal: Xerox let Ethernet and its networking lead diffuse to others while it defended its copier cash cow. It invented how offices would connect and captured almost none of the value.

choices.text: Build the networked-office business you invented.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You commercialize Ethernet and own the plumbing of every connected office. The networking industry grows up inside Xerox instead of around it.

choices.text: License Ethernet broadly to set the standard.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You make Ethernet the universal standard and collect on its adoption everywhere. Xerox becomes synonymous with connectivity, not just copies.

bands.line: You invented the future and actually kept it. Villain episode: WON.
A real computing player alongside the copier business — not a cautionary tale.
You invented the personal computer and stayed a copier company. Faithful to history. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Xerox invented what Apple monetized. Today's agents flagged a company sitting on a breakthrough it refuses to ship →
